ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Lamb and mutton meat dishes) is the closest defensible category for Bhuna Gosht. No exact code exists for this specific preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical main course serving of Bhuna Gosht is approximately 250g, representing a restaurant-style portion of spiced mutton curry served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Bhuna Gosht (Main Course, Mutton) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bhuna Gosht (Main Course, Mutton), cr_id G00010860. All per_100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat-Based Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Spiced Meat Curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bhuna Gosht in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous spiced meat curry dishes with low carbohydrate content (7.44g/100g). The dominant carbohydrate sources are yoghurt and tomato, both of which have low GI values (yoghurt GI ~14–36, tomato GI ~15). Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as: (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5g. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate meat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 1168mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2000mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150g and request reduced-salt preparation; the potassium content (210mg/100g) provides partial offset but is insufficient to neutralise the sodium burden at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 7.44g/100g with sugar at 4.69g/100g; high protein (14.05g/100g) slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response; estimated GI is low at 35.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhuna Gosht is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (150–200g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; monitor total meal glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 11.58g/100g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 4.5g/100g; cholesterol is 85mg/100g; sodium at 467mg/100g is elevated and contributes to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Bhuna Gosht to small portions (100–150g), request reduced oil and salt during preparation, and avoid frequent consumption given the combined saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways central to RA pathogenesis; nightshade ingredients (tomato, red chilli) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise Bhuna Gosht due to the red meat content; consider substituting with chicken, fish, or legume-based alternatives, and monitor tolerance to nightshade-derived ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhuna Gosht is a classic North Indian and Pakistani dry-style mutton curry in which cubed lamb or mutton is marinated in yoghurt and spices, then cooked using the 'bhuna' technique — a method of stir-frying and slow-cooking the meat in its own juices with minimal added liquid until the masala clings tightly to the meat. The dish is characterised by a rich, deeply caramelised spice coating, tender meat, and an intensely savoury flavour profile with notes of cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and red chilli. The yoghurt marinade tenderises the mutton and contributes a subtle tang. In Singapore, Bhuna Gosht is served as a main course in Indian-Muslim restaurants and North Indian eateries, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed rice. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat, and 7.44g carbohydrate, with sodium at 467mg/100g. The high protein content and low carbohydrate profile make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled, but the elevated sodium level requires caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The red meat base is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content.
common_user_queries: Is Bhuna Gosht suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat Bhuna Gosht with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010860) with confidence 0.95. The HPB record is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5g/100g based on typical mutton fat composition as no disaggregated fatty acid data is provided in the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to low-carbohydrate spiced meat dishes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Bhuna Gosht preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010860) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent exact match as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5g/100g based on USDA SR Legacy data for cooked lamb shoulder with spices (USDA FDC ID 174351 used as compositional reference for fatty acid estimation only; primary nutrient values remain HPB-sourced). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bhuna Gosht exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5g. The dish is listed in the HPB database under the name 'Bhuna Gosht (Main Course 主菜 (Mutton 羊肉)' and is classified as a reviewed canonical record.
